title: Establish Rapport
intro:
STEP 1 OF 8 · MODULE 1

Establish Rapport

Mission: Make a friend in the first 90 seconds. Become the human the customer trusts before they trust the shop.

OBJECTIVE
MAKE A FRIEND
TIME WINDOW
90 SECONDS
PRIMARY KPI
GREET→TOUR %
FAILURE MODE
SOUNDS SCRIPTED
Mission Brief The Psychology Three Channels The 90-Sec Window Scripts 5 Mistakes Drills Mastery Coach's Corner KPIs Worksheet

sections.title: Mission Brief
sections.html:
Mission Brief

Rapport is not small talk. Rapport is the moment a stranger’s nervous system relaxes enough to receive information. Every other step in this system — credibility, custody, inspection, presentation, close — is gated by this one. If the customer is still in fight-or-flight when you start talking about a water pump, they will not hear the water pump. They will hear a salesperson.

So the first 90 seconds are not a warm-up. They are the highest-leverage 90 seconds of the entire sale. We spend them deliberately.

A customer in rapport authorizes work. A customer out of rapport authorizes nothing — not even a free inspection.

sections.title: The Psychology — WHY Rapport Works
sections.html:
The Psychology — WHY Rapport Works

When a stranger calls or walks into our shop, their brain runs a sub-second threat assessment in the amygdala. Two questions get answered before any conscious thought: Is this person safe? and Are they like me? If the answer to either is “no,” the prefrontal cortex (the reasoning brain) goes offline and the limbic brain takes over. From that point on, every word out of the advisor’s mouth gets filtered through suspicion.

Rapport flips both answers to “yes” in under 90 seconds — not because we trick the customer, but because we are deliberately producing the signals their nervous system is scanning for. Three mechanisms do the heavy lifting:

MECHANISM 1

Mirror Neurons

Humans unconsciously copy the emotional state of the person in front of them. Smile, and they smile. Use warm tone, and they relax. Tense up at the price quote, and they tense up. The advisor sets the emotional thermostat for the room.

MECHANISM 2

Limbic Resonance

Pace and rhythm sync between two people in conversation. A calm, confident advisor pulls a panicked customer down into calm. A rushed advisor speeds the customer up into hesitation. Pace controls trust.

MECHANISM 3

Reciprocity

Give first — a warm greeting, a glass of water, eye contact, a smile — and the brain creates a tiny obligation. That obligation is what later gets cashed in as “sure, let’s do the inspection.”

PSYCHOLOGY DECODERWhy “You called the right place” lands harder than “Hi, how can I help?”

The brain is scanning for relief. “You called the right place” is a one-sentence relief delivery: it tells the limbic system “you can stop searching, you can stop comparing, the threat is resolved.” The body literally exhales. From that exhale forward, everything we say is being heard by the reasoning brain instead of being filtered by suspicion. This is why we never replace this line with “Sure, we can do that.” “Sure” is information. “Right place” is relief.

sections.title: The Three Channels of Rapport
sections.html:
The Three Channels of Rapport

Rapport is transmitted on three channels simultaneously. If any one is broken, the customer’s nervous system gets a mixed signal and defaults back to suspicion. Coach all three. Score all three.

Figure 1.1 · All three channels carry the signal. Break one, lose the room.
ChannelWhat “On” Looks LikeWhat “Off” Looks LikeHow to Train
Tone Voice slightly higher at the greeting (smile is audible), slows down at “right place,” warm on the customer’s name. Monotone. Reads the script. Sounds like the third call in a row. Record the greeting. Listen back. If you can’t hear the smile, you didn’t smile.
Words “You called the right place.” “Oh wow.” “Of course.” “Absolutely.” First-person ownership: “I’ll personally…” “Sure.” “Yeah.” “Uh, we can do that.” Third-person dodges: “the tech will…” Script-shadowing — trainer says the line, advisor immediately repeats with the same inflection.
Body Stands up at the desk. Eye contact within 2 seconds. Smile precedes words. Open posture toward the customer. Stays seated. Looks at the screen. Greeting comes from behind a monitor. Crossed arms. The “stand-and-smile” drill: every time the front bell rings, stand before you speak.

sections.title: The 90-Second Friendship Window
sections.html:
The 90-Second Friendship Window

The clock starts the instant the phone rings or the front door opens. After 90 seconds, the customer’s first impression locks in — it’s nearly impossible to overturn it later in the call. Use the window deliberately.

Figure 1.2 · The 90-second window — four deliberate moves, one outcome

sections.title: Scripts — The Exact Words
sections.html:
Scripts — The Exact Words

SCRIPTThe Greeting (phone, before ring 3)

You: [SMILE] “Thank you for calling [Shop]. My name is [Your Name].” [PAUSE]

If hesitation: “How can I help you today?”

SCRIPTThe Relief Line

Customer: “Do you work on brakes?”

You: “You called the right place! Is this your first time with our shop?”

SCRIPTThe Arrival (in-person)

You: [Greet at the front door or parking lot, boom board in hand] “Welcome to [Shop]. I’m [Your Name] and I’ll be taking care of you today — may I have your keys, please?”

PSYCHOLOGY DECODERWhy we ask for the keys first.

Asking for the keys is the lightest possible custody request. It signals competence (“I know what I’m doing”), it gives the customer something physical to do with their hands, and it produces a tiny act of compliance — the first “yes” in the relationship. From that yes forward, every subsequent yes is easier. This is the principle of commitment momentum: people stay consistent with their last small action.

PRO TIPUse the customer’s name three times in the first two minutes.

Once on the greeting, once during the concern pivot, once when handing off to the tour. After three uses, the relationship feels personal to the customer’s subconscious. Don’t overdo it — four+ in two minutes flips to creepy.

sections.title: The 5 Common Mistakes That Kill Rapport
sections.html:
The 5 Common Mistakes That Kill Rapport

These are the unforced errors we see on call recordings every single week. Each one breaks one of the three channels. Coach them out before they harden into habits.

#MistakeChannel It BreaksQuick Fix
1Letting the phone ring 4+ times.Tone (signals “you’re an interruption”)2-ring rule. No exceptions.
2Greeting from behind a monitor without eye contact.BodyStand-and-smile drill on every door open.
3Answering the price question in the first 30 seconds.Words (sells before relief lands)Reflex line: “at no cost to you, I’ll personally…”
4Filler words: “um,” “uh,” “like,” “ya know.”Tone (signals uncertainty)The Pause Drill (below). Silence beats filler.
5Skipping the customer’s name entirely.All threeName on the boom board, eyes on the name every callback.

sections.title: Drills — Build the Instinct
sections.html:
Drills — Build the Instinct

DRILL 1.ASmile-Through-Voice (60-second reps)

Setup: Two advisors. One holds a mirror. Other places a real call to the shop and delivers the greeting twice — once with mouth flat, once smiling visibly in the mirror.

Score (1–10): Can the listener hear the smile? Did pace soften on “right place”?

Goal: 8+/10 on three consecutive reps, then graduate to live calls.

DRILL 1.BThe Pause

Setup: Trainer plays customer. Advisor delivers the greeting and must hold full silence for 2 seconds before any further word, even if the customer hesitates.

Score: Pause held? No filler? Did the customer speak first?

Goal: 5 consecutive reps with no filler. This unlocks Level 2.

DRILL 1.CName × 3

Setup: Full simulated call. Advisor must work the customer’s first name into the conversation exactly three times within two minutes — greeting, concern pivot, hand-off.

Score: Three uses? Natural inflection (not pasted on)? Did the customer’s tone soften?

Goal: Land 3/3 on five live calls in a single shift.

DRILL 1.DThe Cold-Open Roleplay (Boss-prep)

Setup: Use the AI roleplay bot in “hostile cold caller” mode — customer opens with “Just tell me how much an oil change costs.” Advisor has 90 seconds to convert to East-West appointment without quoting.

Score: Tone warmth + no-quote discipline + East-West executed + appointment booked.

sections.title: Mastery Check — Gate to Level 2
sections.html:
Mastery Check — Gate to Level 2

LEVEL 1 · RAPPORT MASTERY

  • Picked up before the third ring with a smile audible in the voice.
  • Used the relief line (“right place”) within the first 10 seconds.
  • Paused after the opening line and let the customer speak first.
  • Said the customer’s first name at least 3 times in the first 2 minutes.
  • Did not quote a price during the rapport window.
  • Closed the window with an East-West appointment or warm hand-off.

Pass all 6 across three consecutive live calls → promoted to Advisor (Level 2).

sections.title: Coach’s Corner
sections.html:
Coach’s Corner

COACH’S CORNERHow to coach Rapport on the floor.

Listen for, in this order: (1) the smile in the voice on hello, (2) the pause, (3) the relief line, (4) the name use, (5) no price.

Praise behavior, not outcomes. “You held the pause for a full two seconds — that’s a Level-2 move” lands harder than “great call.” Specific praise rewires faster than generic praise.

Correct one channel at a time. If tone and body are both broken, fix tone first — it’s the highest-leverage and least ego-loaded channel.

Red flag pattern: if an advisor’s greet-to-tour rate drops below 60%, the leak is almost always in Rapport, not in Pricing. Audit the first 90 seconds of three of their last calls. You’ll find it.

sections.title: KPIs — What to Measure
sections.html:
KPIs — What to Measure

Greet-to-Tour %
≥ 80%
Of all first-time visitors, what % accept a shop tour? This is the rapport scoreboard.
2-Ring Compliance
100%
% of inbound calls answered by the second ring. Single most controllable metric in the shop.
Inquiry-to-Appointment %
≥ 65%
% of phone inquiries that book. Rapport is the leading indicator — pricing is downstream.
